name: SmarTrust
title: SmarTrust — Trust & Estate Planning Calculator
metaDescription: Free trust and estate planning calculator. Estimate costs, compare trust types, and understand which estate planning strategy is right for your situation.
metaKeywords: trust calculator, estate planning cost, revocable trust vs irrevocable, estate planning calculator, trust comparison
headline: Estate planning, simplified
subheadline: Compare trust types, estimate costs, and figure out which estate plan fits your situation — in 60 seconds.
aboutContent:
Protect your family's future

Without a proper estate plan, your assets go through probate — a public, expensive, and slow court process. A well-structured trust can avoid probate, reduce estate taxes, protect assets from creditors, and ensure your wishes are carried out exactly as intended.

It's not just for the wealthy

Many people think estate planning is only for millionaires. In reality, anyone with a home, retirement accounts, or children should have at least a basic estate plan. A simple revocable living trust can cost less than a single month's mortgage payment.

Types of trusts

Revocable living trusts are the most common — you maintain control and can change them anytime. Irrevocable trusts offer stronger asset protection and tax benefits but can't be easily modified. Special needs trusts protect benefits eligibility. Charitable trusts reduce taxes while supporting causes you care about.
disclaimer: SmarTrust.co provides educational estimates only. This is not legal or financial advice. Consult a qualified estate planning attorney for your specific situation.

faqs.a: This provides rough estimates for educational purposes. Actual costs vary significantly by state, complexity of your estate, and attorney fees. Use these numbers as a starting point for conversations with an estate planning professional.

faqs.q: Do I need a trust or is a will enough?
faqs.a: A will goes through probate (public, slow, expensive). A trust avoids probate and offers more control over distribution. If you own real estate or have assets over $100,000, a trust is generally recommended. A will alone may be sufficient for simple estates.

faqs.q: How much does setting up a trust cost?
faqs.a: A basic revocable living trust typically costs $1,500-$3,000 with an attorney. Complex trusts (irrevocable, special needs, charitable) can cost $3,000-$10,000+. Online trust services start around $300-$500 but may not be appropriate for complex situations.

faqs.q: What's the difference between revocable and irrevocable?
faqs.a: A revocable trust can be modified or dissolved at any time — you keep full control. An irrevocable trust generally can't be changed once created, but it removes assets from your taxable estate and provides stronger creditor protection. Most people start with a revocable trust.

faqs.q: At what estate size do I need to worry about estate taxes?
faqs.a: The federal estate tax exemption is currently over $13 million per individual. Most estates are not subject to federal estate tax. However, some states have their own estate or inheritance taxes with much lower thresholds. Check your state's rules.
